sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 98, level 2.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 72 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Now the dark gray shape is at rows 36-47, cols 0-14. DOWN still doesn't work. Looking at the image, the dark gray shape no longer overlaps with the yellow shape (yellow is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17, dark gray is at rows 36-47, cols 0-14). The overlap would be at rows 42-47, cols 3-14, but the image shows no dots inside the dark gray shape.
Wait, looking more carefully at the image, the dark gray shape and yellow shape are now side by side with no overlap. The yellow shape is to the right of the dark gray shape.
Let me reconsider. The dark gray shape is at cols 0-14 and the yellow shape is at cols 3-17. They should overlap at cols 3-14. But the rows are:
- Dark gray: 36-47
- Yellow: 42-53
Overlap rows: 42-47
So they should overlap at rows 42-47, cols 3-14.
But the image shows the dark gray shape to the LEFT of the yellow shape, with no overlap. This suggests the dark gray shape might have a different shape than I thought.
Looking at the image more carefully, the dark gray shape appears to be an L-shape that's different from the yellow L-shape. The dark gray L-shape has the long part on the LEFT and the short part on the TOP. The yellow L-shape has the long part on the BOTTOM and the short part on the RIGHT.
Actually, looking at the current image, the dark gray shape is at cols 0-14, which is to the left of the yellow shape at cols 3-17. They should overlap at cols 3-14, rows 42-47. But the image shows no overlap.
Wait, maybe the dark gray shape doesn't extend to rows 42-47 at cols 3-14. Let me look at the shape more carefully.
The dark gray shape is an L-shape. If it's at rows 36-47, cols 0-14, the L-shape might have:
- Vertical part: rows 36-47, cols 0-5 (or similar)
- Horizontal part: rows 42-47, cols 0-14 (or similar)
And the yellow shape is also an L-shape at rows 42-53, cols 3-17:
- Vertical part: rows 42-53, cols 3-8 (or similar)
- Horizontal part: rows 48-53, cols 3-17 (or similar)
So the overlap might be at rows 42-47, cols 3-14, but only if the dark gray shape has pixels there.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem. Let me look at the image again:
- The dark gray shape is now at the far left, not overlapping with the yellow shape.
- The yellow shape is still at its original position.
- The black shape on the right is unchanged.
I've been moving the dark gray shape around but haven't found the winning condition. Let me try a completely different approach.
Maybe the goal is to have the dark gray shape at a specific position where it creates a specific dot pattern. Let me try to systematically explore all possible positions.
But I'm running out of actions. Let me try to be more strategic.
Key observations:
1. The dark gray shape can move UP, DOWN (sometimes), LEFT, RIGHT
2. LEFT/RIGHT moves by 6 cols
3. UP/DOWN moves by 3 rows (when they work)
4. SPACE toggles something (dot color, yellow shape size)
5. ACTION7 moves the shape up by 3 rows
Let me try to map out all possible positions:
- Starting position: rows 36-47, cols 12-26 (after ACTION7)
- LEFT: cols 6-20
- LEFT again: cols 0-14
- RIGHT: cols 12-26 (back to start)
- UP: rows 33-44, 30-41, 27-38, 24-35, 21-32, 18-29
- DOWN: doesn't work at rows 36-47
So the dark gray shape can be at:
- Rows: 18-29, 21-32, 24-35, 27-38, 30-41, 33-44, 36-47
- Cols: 0-14, 6-20, 12-26, 18-32, 24-38 (but 24-38 might go into the divider)
The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17.
To overlap with the yellow shape, the dark gray shape needs to be at rows that include 42-53 and cols that include 3-17.
The lowest row position for the dark gray shape is rows 36-47, which overlaps with rows 42-47 of the yellow shape.
The col positions that overlap with cols 3-17 are: 0-14 (overlap at 3-14), 6-20 (overlap at 6-17), 12-26 (overlap at 12-17).
So the possible overlap positions are:
1. rows 36-47, cols 0-14: overlap at rows 42-47, cols 3-14
2. rows 36-47, cols 6-20: overlap at rows 42-47, cols 6-17
3. rows 36-47, cols 12-26: overlap at rows 42-47, cols 12-17
I already tried position 3 (cols 12-26) and it created dots. Position 2 (cols 6-20) also created dots. Position 1 (cols 0-14) doesn't create dots (no overlap).
Wait, but position 1 should have overlap at rows 42-47, cols 3-14. Let me check if the dark gray shape actually has pixels at those positions.
Actually, looking at the image, the dark gray shape at cols 0-14 doesn't seem to extend to rows 42-47 at cols 3-14. The L-shape might have a specific orientation that prevents overlap at those positions.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of trying to overlap with the yellow shape, let me try to match the black shape's position (mirrored).
The black shape is at rows 39-50, cols 48-62. The mirror across the divider (col 37) would be rows 39-50, cols 12-26.
I already tried this position (rows 39-50, cols 12-26) and it didn't complete the level.
Hmm, let me try pressing SPACE at the mirror position to toggle the dot color.
Actually, I already tried SPACE at various positions and it didn't complete the level.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to have the dark gray shape at the SAME position as the yellow shape (rows 42-53, cols 3-17).
But the dark gray shape can only reach rows 36-47 at most, not rows 42-53.
Wait, maybe I need to use SPACE to change the dark gray shape's size or position. Let me check what SPACE does at the current position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at rows 36-47, cols 0-14
# Try SPACE to see what happens
action(['SPACE'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Yellow: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check dots
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ('S', 'w', 'Y') and 1 < n['pixels'] < 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']} small: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray: row 36-47, col 0-14, pixels=72
Yellow: row 42-53, col 3-17, pixels=72
Y small: row 55-63, col 63-63, pixels=9
Level completed: False
